rdl_hands_forward: Push hips back and drag the bar down your thighs simultaneously: these two cues together keep the bar on the correct path.
Scrape the bar along your thighs on the way down and back up — contact with the legs is the standard for a correct RDL bar path.

Your hands are too far forward from your body during the hinge. In a Romanian deadlift the bar should travel vertically close to your legs. Think about pushing your hips back as the primary movement while actively keeping the bar dragging against your thighs.

Your hands are drifting away from your body during the descent. The bar should graze your legs on the way down and back up. This is one of the most impactful technique points in the Romanian deadlift — keeping the bar close to your body is what allows you to load the hamstrings safely and effectively.
The bar drift is usually a symptom of the hip hinge not being executed correctly. Push your hips back first and let the bar follow your legs naturally.

The bar is drifting forward because the movement is being initiated as a forward bend rather than a hip hinge. When you push your hips back first, your torso tips forward naturally and the bar follows your legs. When you bend forward first, the bar swings away. Lead with the hips.
Keep the bar close enough to scrape your legs: once it drifts forward your lower back takes over from your hamstrings.
One correction to focus on: the bar should not leave your thighs. Fix that and the rest of the movement will follow.

The bar is not staying on your legs during the descent. The broomstick drill provides immediate feedback: hold a broomstick or PVC pipe against your legs while you practice the hinge. The moment the stick leaves your legs you know the bar path has drifted, and you can correct it in real time.

The bar is arcing forward instead of traveling in a straight line close to your body. Use the cue of dragging the bar down your thighs: this mental image naturally keeps the bar on the correct path and increases hamstring tension.

Your hands are drifting too far in front of your body during the Romanian deadlift. The bar path should be vertical and close to your legs, not arcing forward. Try actively thinking about dragging the bar down your thighs as you hinge back.
Drag the bar down your thighs and feel the hamstring tension increase: that tension is the signal the bar is on the right path.

The bar is drifting forward rather than traveling vertically close to your legs. The three elements of correct RDL mechanics are: hips pushing back, back staying flat, and bar dragging along your legs. Right now the third element is missing.
When the bar is in contact with your thighs you can feel your hamstrings working. When it drifts away you lose that feeling. Use that feedback to guide your bar path.
Bar drifting — set up with a breath, core brace, and lat squeeze before every rep to keep the bar close.
Bar drifting away from your body — it should almost scrape your thighs the entire rep.

The bar is drifting forward during the hinge. Combine two images: your hips pushing back toward a wall behind you, and the bar dragging along the front of your legs. These two things together create the correct RDL mechanics and naturally maintain bar contact.
Lat engagement is the mechanical fix for bar drift. Think shoulder blades into back pockets before every rep and maintain that tension throughout.
Three cues together: push hips back, keep back flat, drag bar along your legs. The drift disappears when all three happen.
Your lats are the muscles that keep the bar pinned to your legs. If they are not engaged, the bar will drift. Activate them before every rep.

Your hands are moving away from your body during the Romanian deadlift. Keep the bar close to your legs. A useful drill to practice correct bar path is the wall Romanian deadlift: stand close to a wall and hinge back without letting the bar touch the wall — this forces the bar to stay close to your body.
Feel the difference: one rep with the bar on your legs, one rep with the bar drifting forward. Your hamstrings will tell you which path is correct.
Hinging at the waist sends the bar forward: shift the movement to a hip hinge and the bar naturally follows your legs.
Bar contact with your thighs on the descent and ascent is fundamental: closer is always more efficient and safer.

The bar is losing contact with your legs as you hinge. In a Romanian deadlift the bar path should be nearly vertical and close to your body from start to finish. When it drifts forward the lever arm increases and your lower back absorbs load that should go to your hamstrings.
The daylight test is simple and immediate: no gap between bar and legs means you are on the right path. Any gap and you need to bring it back in.

The bar is leaving your legs because the movement is being initiated at the waist rather than the hips. A waist hinge sends the bar forward. A hip hinge keeps the bar on your legs. Shift the focus to your hips: push them back, and let the bar follow your legs naturally.

The main correction here is bar path: your hands are drifting away from your body instead of staying close to your legs. Think about keeping the bar in contact with your thighs throughout the entire hinge — this is the most important technical cue for the Romanian deadlift.
Bar drifting forward is pulling your weight onto your toes — keep the bar on your legs and your weight back in your heels.
Bar drifting away early — stay in contact with your thighs first, then your shins as you descend.
Imagine a wall in front of you that the bar cannot touch: this cue pulls the bar back toward your legs naturally.
Bar drifting away — close bar path is a standard, not a suggestion. Apply it every single rep.
The hamstrings only get to do their job when the bar stays close to your body. The moment it drifts forward, the lower back takes over. Keep that bar on your legs.

During this Romanian deadlift your hands and the bar are moving away from your legs. Keep the bar as close to your body as possible throughout the movement. A good cue is to think about pulling your hips back while keeping the bar dragging down your thighs.

The bar is traveling forward away from your legs instead of staying close to your body. This is the primary error in this movement. Think of the bar as needing to stay attached to your legs — drag it down and back up along the same path every single rep.

The bar is drifting well away from your legs. Imagine a wall right in front of you that you cannot let the bar touch. This cue naturally pulls the bar back toward your body because you have to keep it from swinging forward into the imaginary wall.

The bar is swinging away from your body as you hinge. Think of it as an object attached to your legs that must stay attached throughout the movement. If you can feel daylight between the bar and your thighs, you have already lost the correct bar path.
Bar leaving your legs during the descent — drag it along your shins and thighs the whole way down.
Maintain bar contact with your legs throughout: the hamstrings need that close bar path to control the load effectively.
In the RDL the bar should almost feel like it is stuck to your legs. Right now there is a gap forming early in the descent. Close that gap and maintain contact.

The bar is arcing forward rather than traveling in a vertical line. A vertical bar path close to your body is the most efficient path in the RDL. Every degree of arc away from vertical adds unnecessary load to your lower back and reduces the tension in your hamstrings.

Bar drift away from your legs removes the hamstrings from the movement and hands the work to your lower back. The hamstrings can only control the load when the bar travels close to your body. Keep it in contact with your thighs and shins and the hamstrings will do their job.
Bar too far from your legs — pretend there is a wall in front of you and keep the bar from touching it.
Set up close to a wall so that if the bar drifts forward it would hit the wall. Then hinge without hitting it. This constraint teaches bar path faster than any verbal cue.
Keep bar contact at your thighs on the upper portion and transition to your shins as you descend: the bar should follow your legs the whole way.
Bar drifting because you are thinking about bending forward — think about pushing your hips back instead.

Bar drift away from the legs is the primary fault in this rep. The Romanian deadlift depends on the bar staying in contact with your thighs throughout the hinge. When it drifts, your hamstrings lose their mechanical advantage and your lower back compensates.

The bar is drifting away from your legs. If you struggle to maintain close bar contact, reduce the load and focus entirely on bar path for several sets. Bar path is a skill that requires focused repetition to ingrain — adding load before the skill is solid will only reinforce the fault.
Thigh contact keeps your hamstrings loaded and your back protected: maintain it throughout the descent.
Keeping the bar close to your legs also prevents shoulder rounding: bar drift and upper back collapse are linked.

Bar drift is the key error here. As the bar moves away from your body the moment arm grows and your lower back has to compensate. Actively pulling the bar into your legs — using your lats to keep it close — fixes this and shifts the load back to where it belongs.

Bar drift away from the legs is the primary fault here. The hamstrings can only effectively control the load when the bar stays close to the body. As it drifts forward the mechanical advantage shifts and the lower back takes over. Maintain contact throughout.

During the descent the bar is moving away from your legs instead of staying close. In a proper Romanian deadlift the bar should remain in contact with your legs throughout — drag it down your shins and thighs. This keeps the load centered and protects your lower back.

The bar is drifting forward during the hinge. Two things need to happen simultaneously: your hips push back and the bar drags along your thighs. When the hips push back correctly the bar naturally wants to stay close. Make both happen deliberately.

The main error is that the bar is drifting away from your legs as you lower it. It should stay close to your body throughout the entire range of motion. Actively think about dragging the bar back up your legs on the ascent as well — this reinforces the correct bar path in both directions.
Engage your lats before the descent by imagining putting your shoulder blades in your back pockets: this pulls the bar into your body and prevents drift.

Your hands are too far in front of your body as you descend. The bar should travel in a straight vertical line close to your legs. When it drifts forward the moment arm increases and the exercise becomes more of a lower back exercise than a hamstring one, which is not the intention.
Bar arcing forward instead of traveling vertically — drag it straight down along your thighs.
Aim for a straight vertical bar path: any arc forward is wasted effort and adds load to your back.
Bar drift transfers load from hamstrings to lower back: keep contact on both the descent and ascent to prevent this.
Try it once keeping the bar in contact with your thighs and compare how your hamstrings feel. The difference is immediate and will teach you the correct bar path better than any cue.

The bar is not close enough to your legs during this Romanian deadlift. It is drifting forward as you descend. Keep the bar dragging down your thighs and shins. This single correction will make the exercise feel more controlled and will shift the load where it belongs — into your hamstrings and glutes.
Focus on dragging the bar along your thighs rather than lowering it: this cue fixes the forward arc and keeps the bar on the right path.
Bar drifting — turn on your lats before you start and keep them engaged to hold the bar close.
The descent and ascent should follow the same track. Right now the bar is leaving the track on the way down. Keep it on your legs throughout and retrace the same path on the way back up.
Bar leaving your legs — push your hips back and drag the bar along your thighs at the same time.
Think of the bar as attached to your legs: drag it down and drag it back up along the same path every rep.
Push the wall behind you with your hips to start the movement. When the hips go back first, the bar stays close. When you bend forward first, it swings away.

The bar is drifting away from your legs. Bar contact with your legs is not something to think about occasionally — it must be a non-negotiable standard on every rep of every set. Build it as a fundamental habit, not an occasional correction.
The hip push-back and the bar-to-leg contact are linked. When you push your hips back correctly the bar naturally wants to stay close to your legs. Let both happen together.

Your hands are swinging forward away from the body during the hinge. The bar should stay as close to your legs as possible throughout the full range of motion. A useful cue: imagine you are trying to keep the bar touching your body from the start to the finish of the movement.

Bar drift during the descent almost always means bar drift during the ascent too. When the bar is off the correct path in one direction it tends to stay off in the other. Fix the bar path on the descent by keeping it grazing your thighs and shins, and the ascent will follow.
Bar drifting away from your legs — this problem gets worse as you get heavier, so correct it now.

The bar is not staying close to your thighs during the descent. Contact between the bar and your thighs is what keeps your hamstrings under tension and your lower back protected. Lose that contact and you lose both the training effect and the safety of the movement.
Bar drift turns the RDL into a lower back exercise instead of a hamstring exercise: keep the bar on a vertical path to train the right muscles.
Bar arcing forward instead of traveling vertically — keep it on a straight line close to your legs.

Bar path is the primary issue. The bar should travel vertically close to your body throughout the hinge, not arc forward. Make keeping the bar in contact with your thighs the single focus of your next set.
Visualize a straight vertical line on the wall and keep the bar on that line throughout the descent. If it arcs, bring it back.

Your hands are drifting forward away from your legs. In a correct Romanian deadlift the bar stays close to the body throughout the movement. Try thinking about the bar path as a straight vertical line — if it is arcing forward you are losing efficiency and loading your back unnecessarily.
Bar drifting forward — your back is compensating because your hamstrings are losing the load. Keep the bar on your legs.

Bar leaving the legs is usually a sign of a faulty hip hinge pattern. When the hip hinge is correct — hips driving back, spine neutral — the bar naturally follows your legs. When you bend forward instead of hinging back, the bar swings away. Lead with the hips.
Bar drifting away — maintain contact at your thighs, then carry that contact to your shins as you go lower.
Feel where your weight is during the descent. If it is on your toes, the bar is too far forward. Push your heels into the floor and drag the bar back to your legs.
The bar path is a symptom of the hip hinge quality. Get the hinge right and the bar path fixes itself.

During this Romanian deadlift the bar is not staying close to your body — it drifts away from your legs as you hinge. Think of your legs as a guide for the bar: on the way down it should trace a path along your thighs and shins, and on the way back up it should follow the same path in reverse.
Bar path is arcing forward — drag it straight down along your legs instead.

The bar is drifting forward away from your legs. The Romanian deadlift requires a vertical bar path that stays close to the body throughout. This keeps the hamstrings under tension and the lower back in a safer position. Drag the bar down your thighs every rep.

Your hands are drifting forward during the Romanian deadlift. The bar should stay close to your legs and move in a vertical path. When the bar drifts forward it also tends to pull your shoulders into a rounded position — keeping it close naturally helps you maintain a better upper back position as well.
Hands drifting away — bar contact with your thighs is the most important cue to fix.

The bar is leaving your legs and drifting forward. Notice also that as the bar drifts it tends to pull your shoulders forward and round your upper back. Keeping the bar close to your legs prevents this chain reaction — good bar path and good upper back position are linked.
The bar should be close enough to graze your shins: if it is not, it is too far forward.
Bar leaving your legs — treat it as attached and drag it along your legs in both directions.
Bar drifting away from your thighs — keep it close enough to almost touch them the whole way down.
Bar drifting forward is pulling your shoulders into a rounded position — fix the bar path and the upper back organizes itself.
Think of the descent in two phases: thigh contact first, then shin contact as you get lower. Right now you are losing both early in the movement.
Bar drifting forward and overloading your lower back — keep it on a straight vertical path close to your legs.

The bar is leaving your legs and drifting forward. Notice also that this tends to pull your weight onto your toes. The bar drifting forward and the weight shifting to your toes are the same problem: both are caused by bending forward instead of hinging back. Keep the bar on your legs and your weight in your heels.
Push hips back while dragging the bar against your thighs: these two happen together in a correct RDL.

The bar is losing contact with your legs during the hinge. Combine two cues: push your hips back as the primary movement, and simultaneously drag the bar down your thighs. These two actions work together to keep the bar close and the hamstrings loaded.

Your hands are drifting forward away from your body. The bar should stay in contact with your legs throughout the movement. When it moves away from the body it also tends to shift your weight forward onto your toes — check that your weight stays back in your heels as you hinge.
Bar swinging forward — keep it close to your body and your center of mass stays stable.
Close contact between the bar and your legs is not optional in the RDL — it is what makes the movement work. Keep it dragging along your thighs from top to bottom.
The difference between a hip hinge and a waist hinge is exactly the difference between the bar staying on your legs and the bar drifting forward. Make it a hip hinge.
Too much space between the bar and your legs — keep it grazing your thighs the whole descent.

Your hands are swinging away from your body during the Romanian deadlift. The bar path should be vertical and close to your legs. If the bar drifts forward your center of mass shifts and your lower back has to work much harder to compensate. Keep it close.
Push hips back toward a wall while dragging the bar along the front of your legs: these two images together give you the correct RDL.
Bar drifting — if you can see space between the bar and your legs, it is already too far away.

The bar is not staying close to your legs during the descent — it drifts forward. Keep the bar in contact with your thighs and shins throughout the movement. The bar staying close to your body is what allows the hamstrings to do the work they are supposed to do in this exercise.
Watch how your lower back tightens as the bar drifts away. That tightening is a warning sign. Keep the bar close to your legs and that tightening disappears.

The bar is drifting away from your legs during the descent. This is a mechanical error that shifts load from your hamstrings to your lower back. Focus on keeping the bar in contact with your body throughout the movement — on the way down and on the way back up.

The bar is moving away from your legs during the descent instead of staying close. In the Romanian deadlift the bar should essentially scrape your legs from top to bottom. Losing contact with the body is a sign that the hip hinge is not being executed correctly — focus on pushing the hips back first.

The bar is leaving your legs during the hinge. Think of your legs as a rail that the bar runs along: down the thighs and shins on the descent, and back up the same path on the ascent. The bar should follow the same rail in both directions.
Grazing your legs with the bar is not optional — it is the core technique requirement of the RDL.

Your hands are moving away from your legs during the hinge. The bar should travel in a vertical path close to your body. If you consistently struggle with bar drift, try practicing the Romanian deadlift with a light kettlebell held against your legs — this trains the correct bar path very effectively.

The bar is drifting forward away from your legs. Lat engagement is the mechanical solution: when your lats are active they pull the bar into your body and prevent the forward drift. Engage them before the first rep and maintain that engagement throughout the entire set.

The bar is leaving your legs and drifting forward during the hinge. When this happens the load shifts from your hamstrings — where it belongs — to your lower back, which has to work much harder to compensate. Keep the bar grazing your legs to maintain the correct load distribution.

Your hands are drifting away from your legs during the hinge. The bar should stay close to your body at all times. One way to reinforce this is to actively think about dragging the bar back up your legs during the ascent — if you focus on the concentric phase it will also improve your bar path on the way down.

Your hands are moving too far forward from your body during the Romanian deadlift. The bar should graze your legs on the way down and back up. If the bar drifts forward it shifts the load from your hamstrings to your lower back, which is both less effective and more risky.
Bar too far from your legs — close the gap until it would graze your shins on the way down.
Bar too far forward — hips push back, bar drags along your thighs. Both at the same time.

The bar is not staying close to your legs during this Romanian deadlift — it drifts forward during the descent. Think about engaging your lats before and during the movement to keep the bar pulled in close. Lat engagement is what prevents the bar from drifting away from your body.
Use the broomstick drill: hold a stick against your legs while you hinge and feel immediately when it drifts away.
Every rep should end with the bar having grazed your legs the whole way down and back up. If it left your legs at any point during the rep, the rep had a fault.
Think of the bar path as a relay: thighs for the first half, shins for the second. Right now you are handing off early and losing contact entirely.
Try the wall RDL drill: stand close to a wall and hinge without letting the bar touch it — the constraint forces correct bar path.

Your hands are too far in front of your legs during the hinge. The bar should stay very close to your body. Try this cue: before you start the descent, engage your lats by thinking about putting your shoulder blades in your back pockets. This keeps the bar pulled in close throughout the movement.
Bar drifting on the way down — focus on dragging it up your legs on the ascent and the descent will improve.

The bar is arcing forward. Change the mental model: instead of thinking about bending forward, think about pushing your hips backward. When your mental focus is on the hips going back rather than the torso going forward, the bar naturally follows your legs and stays on the correct path.
Drag the bar down your thighs on every rep to maintain the required vertical bar path.

The bar is not staying close to your body during this Romanian deadlift — it drifts forward as you hinge. Keep it close to your legs throughout the movement. Try slowing down the descent and focusing entirely on bar path before adding more weight.

The bar is leaving your legs rather than grazing them. Leg contact is not a minor detail in the RDL — it is the fundamental technique requirement that makes the exercise work correctly. When the bar grazes your legs your hamstrings are loaded and your back is safe. When it drifts, neither is true.
Bar drift during descent usually means drift during ascent too: fix the path by keeping the bar grazing your legs in both directions.
Bar drifting every rep — drag it along your legs in both directions and feel the whole exercise transform.
Bar swinging away from your body — keep it in contact with your legs the entire movement.
Bar drift becomes exponentially more dangerous as weight increases: fix the bar path now before adding load.
Reframe the movement as pushing your hips back rather than bending forward: this mental shift naturally brings the bar to your legs.

The bar is drifting forward away from your legs. In a Romanian deadlift the bar should stay close to your body from start to finish. Try thinking about the movement as a backward push of the hips rather than a forward bend — this mental shift often naturally brings the bar closer to the body.
Think of your legs as a rail: the bar runs down your thighs and shins on the descent and back up the same path on the ascent.
Bar leaving your legs — keep it in contact throughout the full range of motion.

The bar is drifting forward rather than staying on your legs. Dragging the bar along your thighs and shins is not just a safety cue — it is what makes the exercise feel controlled and what shifts the load to your hamstrings and glutes where it belongs.

The bar is drifting forward during the hinge. The kettlebell drill is excellent for teaching the correct bar path: hold a kettlebell by the horns and press it against your legs as you hinge. The kettlebell's shape and weight make it naturally want to stay close, which teaches your body the correct feel.
A simple test: if you could wear shorts and the bar would not touch your legs on the way down, the bar is in the wrong place. Get it close enough to make contact.
Keep the bar dragging along your thighs and shins: a near-vertical bar path close to your body is what makes this exercise safe and effective.
The bar should feel like it is being dragged along your skin, not swinging freely in front of you. Keep it attached to your legs from start to finish.
Bar drifting away — use your legs as a guide rail for the bar in both directions.
A bar that drifts at this weight will drift even more with heavier loads. The time to fix bar path is at lighter weights, not after you have added more.

The bar is leaving your legs during the hinge. Contact between the bar and your thighs on both the descent and ascent is fundamental to the RDL. Efficiency and safety both depend on how close the bar stays to your body — closer is always better.

Your hands are moving away from your body as you hinge. Keep the bar in contact with your thighs throughout the descent and ascent. This is a fundamental technique point in the Romanian deadlift — the closer the bar is to your body, the more efficient and safe the movement is.

The bar is leaving your legs during the descent. This fault compounds with load: the heavier the bar, the more dangerous and uncontrollable a forward drift becomes. Fix the bar path now at this weight so that when you add load the movement remains safe and effective.

The bar is drifting away from your legs during this Romanian deadlift. It should stay close to your body. A cue that helps many people: before starting the descent, take a deep breath, brace your core, and actively squeeze your lats to pull the bar into your body — maintain that lat tension throughout the entire movement.
rdl_head_position: Chin elevated and head in extension. Bring your chin to neutral — not tucked hard, not lifted high, just neutral — and let it follow the angle of your torso. Your neck will feel noticeably more relaxed.

Your head is too elevated during this RDL. The correct position is for your head to be an extension of your neutral spine — wherever your torso goes, your head follows without any extra extension at the neck.
Instead of picking a point on the wall to stare at, find a spot on the floor a metre or so ahead. Your neck will relax and align with your spine automatically.
A relaxed neck is a neutral neck. Right now your neck is not relaxed — it is holding your head up. Let it go.
Think of your head as the final piece of your neutral spine. Every other piece is in the right position — extend that neutrality through your neck and to the top of your head.

The chin is lifting throughout the hinge rather than following the torso. Think of your chin and chest as connected: as your chest tips forward and down, your chin should come with it, not strain upward.
Let your gaze move with your torso: as you tip forward, your eyes should follow rather than staying on the horizon.
Gaze on the wall — let your spine lead and your gaze will follow to the floor ahead.

You are keeping your head up and looking forward during this Romanian deadlift. As you hinge, your head should follow your torso downward — keep your neck neutral and let your gaze follow the floor rather than the horizon.
Your head is in extension throughout this hinge with your chin lifted. Keep your head aligned with your spine by softening your neck and letting your head follow the natural direction of your torso.
Your spine is angling forward but your gaze is still on the wall. Let your head follow your spine and your gaze will naturally drop to track the floor ahead.
Gaze fixed on the horizon — let your eyes drop with the torso as you hinge.
Bring your chin to neutral: it should travel with your torso during the hinge, not stay elevated.
Your head is the top of a neutral spine: let your chin come to neutral and your eyes follow your torso angle.

You are keeping your head up and looking forward during the Romanian deadlift. Instead, allow your head to move naturally with your torso so your neck stays in a neutral position throughout the movement.
Head held up and gaze fixed forward. In the RDL your head should be passive — just going where your spine takes it, with your gaze naturally tracking the floor as you hinge. No active holding.
Your neck is extended too much during the RDL, with your head held high. Your gaze should track the floor a couple of metres ahead of you as you hinge, keeping your head and spine aligned.
Head staying forward and up — let it follow your torso so your neck stays neutral.
Stop actively holding your head up. Let it be passive and just follow wherever your spine goes. Your neck should feel relaxed throughout the hinge.
Your neck is extended and your head is elevated throughout this Romanian deadlift. Keep your neck neutral by letting your head follow your spine — no extra extension at the neck during the hinge.

Your head is being held up during the RDL, looking too far forward. Your head should remain in line with your torso throughout the hinge — keep your gaze directed toward the floor a few feet in front of you rather than straight ahead.
At the bottom of your hinge, find a spot on the floor a metre in front of you and look at it. That gaze direction will put your head in the correct neutral position relative to your spine.

During this Romanian deadlift your head is elevated with your gaze fixed straight ahead. Let your gaze drop as you hinge — your head should follow your torso so that your neck stays relaxed and neutral throughout.

Forward gaze during the RDL puts the neck into extension. Soften your eyes and direct them at the floor a short distance ahead — your head will naturally find a neutral position relative to your spine.

A level gaze during an RDL means your neck is in extension. As your torso tips forward during the hinge, your eyes should follow it toward the floor. Do not try to maintain a level horizon — let your head go with the movement.
Chin elevated — lower to neutral and let your head follow your spine direction.
Your torso is going down and forward. Let your head come along. Drop your gaze to the floor and your head will follow naturally.

Your head is being held up with your chin elevated during this hinge. Keep your head in line with your spine — your chin should be neutral, not lifted, and your head should follow the direction your torso is going.
Chin reaching up during the hinge — let it come down with your chest rather than staying up.
Head staying upright as torso becomes horizontal — match your head angle to your torso angle.
Soften your neck and let your eyes follow your torso toward the floor: stop holding your head elevated.

You are looking too far forward during this hinge. Your gaze should be directed at the floor a few feet ahead of you, not at the wall. Let your head follow your spine and your gaze will naturally find the right direction.
Think of your spine as a straight line that goes all the way through your neck and into your head. Maintain that line during the hinge without bending the neck backward.
Chin up and neck strained — bring your chin neutral and let it follow your torso.
Neutral chin, neutral neck: your head follows the direction your spine is pointing throughout the hinge.
Soften your neck and your head finds neutral automatically: the gaze drops from wall to floor as a result.

Head in extension and gaze too far forward. As you hinge, your gaze should naturally travel with your torso — from a more forward direction at the top to a more floor-directed angle at the bottom. Let that natural movement happen.
Head held up actively — let it be passive and follow the spine.

The gaze is too far forward and the neck is in extension. Tracking the floor a couple of metres ahead gives you a natural target that will bring your head into the correct position without having to think about the neck directly.
A neutral spine means neutral from your tailbone to the top of your head. Right now the top of your spine is extended while the rest is neutral. Bring your head into line.

Extended neck and head elevated throughout. Softening your neck is the primary cue — when the neck softens, the head finds neutral automatically and your gaze drops naturally toward the floor rather than staying on the wall.
Your head is not in line with your spine during this hinge — it is elevated with your chin lifted. Let your neck relax and allow your head to be carried naturally by the movement of your spine.

You are hyperextending your neck during this hinge by looking too far up. Your cervical spine should remain neutral — let your head be a passive extension of your spine rather than actively looking forward.

Neck hyperextension during the RDL strains the cervical vertebrae and breaks the neutral spine position. Your head should be passive — it goes where the spine goes, without actively looking up or forward.
Let your head hinge with your torso: it is part of your spine and should go where the rest of it goes.
Your torso is doing the right thing. Your head just needs to come along for the ride. Relax your neck and let your head follow your torso as a single unit.
Track the floor with your eyes as you hinge: your head will follow your torso into the correct alignment.
Soften your neck and drop your gaze to the floor a couple of metres ahead. Both changes happen at the same time and your head position corrects itself.
Chin elevated throughout the movement. Lower your chin to a neutral position — not tucked aggressively, just neutral — and let your head follow the direction of your spine as you hinge.

Head up and horizon gaze during the hinge. Your head needs to follow your torso as it tips forward. Keep the neck neutral, drop the gaze to the floor, and your head will naturally align with the rest of your spine.
Relax your neck and let your head be carried by your torso: stop actively holding it forward.
Head not part of the neutral spine — bring your chin to neutral and let your head follow the rest of your spine.
Head held up actively — let it be passive and sit on top of your spine naturally.

Your torso is hinging but your head is not following. This mismatch creates unnecessary tension and shear at the cervical spine. Let your head tip forward with your torso — it should be part of the same movement.

During this Romanian deadlift your head is elevated and your gaze is fixed forward. As you hinge, let your eyes track toward the floor in front of you — your head will naturally follow and align with your spine.
Let your eyes follow the floor as your torso tips forward: do not try to maintain a level horizon gaze.
Looking too far forward — soften your gaze and let it drop with the movement.

The neck is working to hold the head up rather than remaining neutral. In a correct RDL the head is passive — it follows the spine without any active lifting. Relax your neck and let your head go with your torso.

During this RDL your head is elevated and your gaze is fixed straight ahead. As you hinge, your eyes should follow the floor rather than staying level with the horizon. Let your head travel with your spine.
Neck hyperextended — your head needs to follow your spine, not strain upward.

rdl_too_much_depth: in the RDL, appropriate depth is determined by how far your hips can push back. stop right at that point, before your lower back steps in to compensate.
you are taking the RDL further than it should go. stop the movement when your hips can no longer push back.
in the Romanian deadlift you should stop when your hips have fully pushed back. you are continuing past that point, so end the rep before your lower back rounds.
you are taking the RDL deeper than your hip hinge can support. end the movement when your hips can no longer travel backward, and stop before your lower back starts to round.
too much depth here. stop the bar when your hips have fully pushed back, before your lower back compensates.

stop when your hamstrings are fully loaded and your hips can no longer travel back. that is the natural end of the RDL, and continuing past it means your lower back has to compensate for the extra range.
you are going too far in this RDL. stop the descent when your hips can no longer push back.
stop when your hips have pushed back as far as they can go. continuing past that is what leads to lower back compensation on this lift.
stop when your hip hinge gives out. going past that point is where the error is, so use that as your end position.
you are going past the correct depth in this RDL. stop when your hips can no longer hinge back, before your lower back rounds.
that is too far down. end the rep when your hips have hit their limit and can no longer push back, right before your lower back rounds.
stop when your hips have pushed back as far as possible. going past that is the error to fix on this lift.
the end point of this lift is when your hamstrings hit full stretch and your hips run out of hinge range. you are pushing past that, which forces your lower back to compensate and puts it at risk.
you are passing the correct stopping point. when your hips have reached maximum backward travel, that is where the rep ends.
your hips reaching their push-back limit is your signal to stop. end the rep there, before your lower back steps in to compensate.
the movement should stop at the point where your hips can no longer hinge backward. you are going past that into lower back compensation.
you are going past the correct end of this RDL. stop when your hips can no longer push back, before your lower back starts to compensate.
that is too deep. the movement should end when your hips can no longer push back, which is well before your lower back begins to round.

you are going further than the Romanian deadlift is designed to take you. stop when your hamstrings are fully stretched and your hips can no longer hinge back, because past that point your lower back is the only thing keeping the bar moving.
your bottom position is when your hips have pushed back as far as they can go. end the rep there, before your lower back starts to round.
stop when your hips can no longer hinge backward. going past that point leads to lower back rounding, which is exactly the problem you have here.
that is too much range. end the rep when your hips have reached maximum backward travel, and hold that as your bottom position.
stop when your hip hinge runs out. going past that point leads to lower back rounding, which is exactly what you are doing.
stop when your hip hinge gives out. going past that point forces your lower back to compensate, and that is the problem you need to fix.

Think of parallel depth as the point where the squat actually begins to train your glutes. Everything above that is mostly quad work with disproportionate knee stress.
The weight is winning over the range of motion. That is the wrong trade. Drop the load until you can consistently reach parallel, and only add weight when the depth is no longer compromised.
Good squat technically with two corrections: you are not reaching full depth, your femurs stop short of parallel, the form is there, just commit to going lower.
Consistently stopping above parallel builds the habit of partial squatting. Reset your standard: every rep must reach parallel before it counts.

You are not descending far enough. The benchmark for correct squat depth is your hip crease: it must drop to at least the level of your kneecap before you reverse the movement. Practice hitting that specific landmark on every rep.
Priority one is depth: descend until your femurs reach parallel on every rep. Do not reverse before hitting that standard.

You are squatting too shallow: the femurs are above parallel and your hips never reach the height of your knees. This significantly reduces the range of motion and limits the effectiveness of the exercise. Work on reaching full depth.
Descend further: your femurs need to reach parallel before you start coming back up.

Prioritize the depth first: get your femurs to parallel on every rep. While doing that, soften your gaze downward rather than staring straight ahead — your head will follow and stay in a more neutral position.
Your squat is too shallow, the femurs are above parallel, try holding a light counterweight in front of you to help you sit deeper.
If you feel unstable going deeper, hold a light weight in front or grip a rack: this shifts your balance and lets you reach parallel.

Squat much deeper: your femurs need to reach parallel with the floor before you reverse the movement. Right now you are only doing a quarter squat and missing most of the range of motion. Drop your hips all the way down until your hip crease reaches your kneecap level.
Drive your knees out hard as you descend: this opens the hips and lets you reach parallel depth.
Not deep enough — drive your knees outward and sit lower until your femurs are parallel.
Partial squats build the habit of partial squatting. Reset the standard: every rep goes to parallel, every single time, regardless of the load.

Your squat stops short of parallel depth. Set a box at the height where your femurs would be parallel to the floor and practice squatting to it with a slow controlled descent. This gives you a tactile target and trains you to reach the correct depth consistently.
Use your kneecap as the depth target: your hip crease should drop to that level or below before you start ascending. Right now you are reversing well before reaching it.
You are close but not quite there. Extend the descent by a few centimeters on each rep until your femurs become horizontal, and you will have the correct depth.
Your squat depth is too high, a useful cue is to think about sitting between your legs rather than straight down, this encourages your hips to drop lower and makes reaching parallel easier.
The hip crease to kneecap benchmark is non-negotiable for a correct squat. Check for it on every rep and do not initiate the drive until you have hit it.

Depth is a skill that requires practice at low intensity. Spend time each session squatting with just bodyweight or an empty bar, committing to full depth every rep, until the correct bottom position feels normal.
If you are stopping short because the weight feels heavy, that is your signal to reduce the load and rebuild the movement at the correct depth. Weight without depth is not progress.

Two corrections needed: first, you are stopping above parallel, so descend further until your femurs become horizontal. Second, your head is tilted back with your gaze fixed straight ahead. Let your eyes drop slightly so your head stays aligned with your spine throughout the movement.

Partial squats do not distribute load the way full squats do. When you stop above parallel, the quads absorb most of the work while the glutes and hamstrings contribute very little. Reach parallel depth to properly share the load across the entire lower body.
Your squat is too shallow, the femurs do not reach parallel — focus on sitting deeper into the movement.
Wait until your hip crease drops to your kneecap level before reversing — you are coming up too early.

Your squat is not reaching parallel. This is especially common when the load increases, as lifters tend to stop slightly higher to manage the weight. If full depth requires reducing the load, reduce it — a partial squat with heavy weight is less effective and less safe than a full squat with appropriate load.
Depth needs to come before load. If you cannot consistently reach parallel with bodyweight, the answer is not to add weight — practice the full range of motion until it is natural.

You are reversing the movement far too early. Think of it as sitting between your legs rather than just bending your knees slightly, and keep descending until you feel your hips drop below knee height.
Almost no squat happening here — your femurs are nearly vertical. Strip the bar and practice the full range of motion with bodyweight first.

Your squat has three things to fix: the depth — your femurs do not reach parallel so focus on squatting deeper. Your hand placement on the bar is slightly too wide, bring it in closer to your shoulders. And your head is a bit too elevated with your gaze fixed straight ahead, lower your eyes slightly, your head should be aligned with your torso rather than tilted back.

Your femurs stop above parallel at the bottom of this squat. A useful training tool is the box squat: set a box at parallel height and practice squatting to it with control, making contact before driving back up. This teaches your body what the correct bottom position feels like.
Too shallow — your femurs need to reach parallel. Just sit lower.
Femurs above horizontal throughout — commit to reaching parallel on every single rep without exception.
Use your kneecap as the depth target: your hip crease should drop to that level or below before you reverse.
Your squat depth is too high: the femurs are well above parallel at the lowest point.
Each rep should end with your femurs horizontal before you reverse. Right now you are stopping several degrees short. Keep the descent going.
Too shallow — slow your descent to four to five seconds and focus on hitting parallel at the bottom.
Everything in your squat looks organized. Now trust that organization and keep descending until your femurs are horizontal — you have the form to support full depth, just use it.

Your squat does not reach parallel depth. The femurs remain above horizontal throughout. Use bodyweight or very light load to practice hitting full depth consistently — the goal is to make the correct range of motion feel automatic before returning to heavier loads.

You are stopping short of parallel. If the reason is that you feel unstable when descending further, a counterweight held in front of the chest solves this immediately. The forward weight balances your center of gravity so you can sit deeper without tipping back.

The squat is too shallow: femurs are not reaching parallel. One helpful mental cue is to think about sitting down to a chair that is just slightly lower than you are comfortable with. This encourages a deeper descent while keeping a controlled movement.
Main issue: too shallow. Femurs need to reach parallel before you start coming back up.
Well above parallel — you need to sit much lower to get the full training effect.

Your squat does not reach parallel depth. If you feel like you are going to fall backward when you try to go deeper, try holding a light counterweight in front of you or holding onto a rack. This shifts your center of gravity forward and makes it easier to sit into a deeper squat.
Depth is a habit. On your next set, consciously hold the descent for a half-second longer and check that your femurs have reached parallel before driving back up.
Your hips need to drop to knee height: the squat's training effect depends on completing the full range of motion.

This movement does not resemble a squat. Your body remains almost completely upright and no meaningful descent is happening. Start from scratch: practice sitting all the way down in a bodyweight squat until you understand what the full movement feels like.
Femurs above parallel — use your hip crease level relative to your knee as the depth checkpoint.

You are not reaching the correct depth. Use your hip crease as the benchmark: at the bottom of every rep it should be at or below the level of your kneecap. Practice hitting that standard consistently with your current load before increasing weight.

A controlled box squat teaches you two things: where parallel depth actually is, and how to sit back into the squat rather than just bending your knees. Use it as a regular drill until full depth becomes automatic.

If you are unsure of what parallel depth looks like, set a box or bench so that when you touch it lightly with your hips, your femurs are horizontal. Squat to that target consistently until you internalize the depth.
You are not reaching parallel depth in this squat, if mobility is not the limiting factor, focus on building the habit of sitting lower before reversing.

The fix for shallow depth is often as simple as opening your hips more on the way down. Think about spreading your knees apart aggressively as you descend, and you will find you can sit noticeably lower.
You are not going deep enough, the femurs do not reach parallel, commit to a deeper descent.
Reach parallel: your femurs need to become horizontal at the bottom before you drive back up.
Weakness at the bottom of a squat is fixed by training at the bottom. Add two-second pauses at parallel depth to build comfort and strength in the position you are currently skipping.
Push your knees out as you descend to create hip space, and keep going until your femurs reach parallel before reversing.
You are not reaching parallel depth. Your femurs stop above horizontal at the bottom and you are missing the deepest portion of the squat range of motion.
The depth of this squat is not sufficient, your femurs do not reach parallel, try squatting to a low box or bench to get a feel for the correct depth.
If the bottom position feels weak, use pause squats: descend to parallel, hold for two to three seconds, then drive back up. This builds strength where you need it.
This squat is too shallow: you are stopping above parallel. Descend further until your femurs are at least parallel with the ground before reversing the movement.
Your squat is too shallow, the femurs do not reach parallel.
External hip rotation creates hip space. The more you drive your knees apart as you sit down, the lower your hips can travel. Use that to get your femurs to horizontal.
Hold a light counterweight in front of your chest and practice sitting deeper: the forward weight shift makes parallel depth much easier to reach.

This is an extremely shallow squat. Your hips drop only a few centimeters and your femurs remain almost completely upright. There is no point in adding load to a movement this far from the correct range of motion. Strip the bar back to just the empty bar or switch to bodyweight, and focus exclusively on learning what full depth actually feels like before progressing.
Femurs above parallel — commit to reaching full depth on every training rep.
Femurs above horizontal — if it is a strength issue at the bottom, pause squats will fix it.
Femurs above parallel — add a box at the correct height to learn where the bottom should be.
Good mechanics — now commit to the full range. Your femurs need a few more centimeters of descent to reach parallel.
Femurs above parallel — use a box at the right height to identify and practice the correct depth.
Fix three things: squat deeper to reach parallel, narrow your grip toward your shoulders, and drop your gaze slightly so your head aligns with your torso.
Way too shallow — your hips barely move and your femurs stay nearly vertical. Get all the way down to parallel.

You are stopping short of parallel depth. Imagine there is a chair set just below where you normally stop — sit all the way down to that imaginary target before reversing. This mental cue often adds the few centimeters of depth needed to reach parallel.
Sit lower: build the habit of descending to parallel before reversing. If mobility is not the issue, this is simply about committing to the full range.
Too shallow — femurs above parallel. Sit lower.

You are reversing the movement before reaching parallel depth. The standard for a correct squat is that your hip crease reaches at least the level of your kneecap at the bottom. Think about spreading the floor with your feet and sitting your hips down between your legs rather than straight down.
Too shallow — drop your hips further until your femurs are level with the ground.

If you feel like you will fall backward when you try to go deeper, holding a small weight in front of you acts as a counterbalance and allows you to drop your hips further. Use this as a learning tool until you can hit depth without it.
The squat is not deep enough, your femurs stay above parallel, work on reaching deeper.
Well above parallel — your hips need to travel much further down before you reverse.

The hips barely move in this squat. A few centimeters of descent is not a squat — it is a partial knee bend with no useful training effect. Remove the load and spend time in a deep bodyweight squat, getting comfortable with your hips below your knees, before returning to any loaded version.

Shallow squat: your femurs are above parallel at the bottom of the movement. This is a very common error especially when lifting heavier loads. Make sure you are not sacrificing depth for weight — better to squat lighter with correct depth than heavier with a partial range of motion.
Keep descending until your femurs are horizontal: do not reverse before reaching that point.
You are not squatting to parallel, stopping short shifts more load onto the knees and limits posterior chain involvement, try to sit deeper until your femurs are at least parallel with the floor.
Reach parallel to involve your glutes and hamstrings properly. Stopping short puts excessive strain on the knees and leaves the posterior chain barely working.
Practice deep bodyweight squats until full depth feels comfortable, then apply the same range to loaded squats.
The bottom of a correct squat is when your femurs are horizontal. You are reversing several degrees above that. Keep going.
Descend much further: your femurs are well above parallel and you need to keep going until they reach horizontal.
The bottom of a correct squat is where your femurs are parallel with the floor. You are stopping several centimeters before that point. Keep going.
Depth before load: reduce the weight if needed to reach parallel consistently on every rep.
Femurs above parallel — you need to go deeper consistently.

You are stopping the descent too early, the femurs are above parallel. Try the cue of pushing your knees outward over your toes as you descend — this opens up the hips and creates the space needed for your hips to drop to parallel depth.

The depth of this squat is insufficient: femurs are above parallel at the bottom. Reaching full depth is essential for proper squat mechanics and maximum muscle development. Practice reaching deeper with bodyweight before adding load.
Femurs above horizontal — practice deep bodyweight squats until the full range of motion becomes automatic.
